stem: You are the obstetrics registrar called for ongoing atonic PPH after a vaginal delivery in a 33-year-old G2P2. The patient had pre-eclampsia in this pregnancy with peak BP 160/105 on the day of delivery. She has received 10 IU oxytocin IM at delivery and then 40 IU oxytocin in 1 L Hartmann's by infusion. Her BP is now 154/96. Estimated blood loss is 1200 mL and ongoing. She has asthma well controlled by salbutamol PRN with no recent flares. The bladder is empty and the uterus is boggy on bimanual.

data_table.Examination: Boggy uterus to umbilicus, ongoing PV loss, no perineal trauma, no retained placenta on inspection
data_table.Medications: Salbutamol PRN; recent labetalol 200 mg in labour for BP control

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next uterotonic to give?

options.text: Misoprostol 800 micrograms sublingually as second-line uterotonic for ongoing atonic PPH
options.rationale: Misoprostol is the safest second-line uterotonic when ergometrine (HTN) and carboprost (asthma) are contraindicated; 800 mcg sublingual or 1000 mcg rectal is the recommended dose. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Guideline)

options.text: Carboprost 250 micrograms IM as a second-line uterotonic for ongoing atonic PPH now
options.rationale: Carboprost is contraindicated in active asthma; while her asthma is well controlled, the alternative misoprostol is safer in this combined asthma + pre-eclampsia profile. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Guideline)

options.text: Ergometrine 250 micrograms IM as a second-line uterotonic for ongoing atonic PPH now
options.rationale: Ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertension and pre-eclampsia due to severe vasoconstriction; her BP is 154/96 with active pre-eclampsia. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Guideline)

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV slowly with no additional uterotonic until atony reassessed
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid is recommended adjunctively in PPH and should be given (WOMAN trial), but uterotonic for ongoing atony is still required; TXA is adjunct, not replacement. (Source: WOMAN trial; RANZCOG PPH Guideline)

options.text: Repeat oxytocin 10 IU IM and continue oxytocin infusion at 40 IU per litre Hartmann's now
options.rationale: She has already received maximal first-line oxytocin without effect; escalation to second-line uterotonic is required, not repeat first-line. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Guideline)

explanation.summary: PPH management follows the four T's: Tone (uterotonics, bimanual compression), Trauma (inspect for genital tract injury), Tissue (retained placenta), Thrombin (coagulopathy). First-line uterotonic is oxytocin; second-line options are ergometrine (contraindicated in HTN), carboprost (contraindicated in asthma), or misoprostol (broad safety profile). Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is recommended early as adjunct (WOMAN trial). Escalation includes intrauterine balloon tamponade (Bakri), uterine artery embolisation, and surgical options (B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy). Massive transfusion protocol should be activated for ongoing bleeding >1500 mL. Maternal vitals, urine output and ongoing loss must be monitored continuously.
key_points: First-line uterotonic: oxytocin (IM/IV)
Ergometrine: avoid in HTN/pre-eclampsia
Carboprost: avoid in asthma
Misoprostol: safest when others contraindicated (800 mcg SL or 1000 mcg PR)
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV early (WOMAN trial)
explanation.pearls: TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours of PPH onset reduces death from bleeding by about 30% (WOMAN trial); efficacy falls sharply after 3 hours.

stem: You are the obstetrics registrar called for shoulder dystocia in a 28-year-old G2P1. She is fully dilated and the foetal head has delivered but the anterior shoulder will not deliver despite gentle traction. Estimated foetal weight at last scan was 4200 g. She has GDM on insulin. There is no maternal exhaustion or epidural. Last documented foetal heart rate two minutes ago was 140 bpm. Help has been called and the senior registrar will be in 90 seconds.

data_table.Examination: Head delivered, no shoulder rotation, turtle sign present, no cord visible
data_table.Investigations: Recent FBC normal; group and hold valid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next manoeuvre?

options.text: Zavanelli manoeuvre with cephalic replacement followed by urgent emergency caesarean now
options.rationale: Zavanelli is last resort after multiple manoeuvres have failed and carries high maternal morbidity; not a first step. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: McRoberts manoeuvre with suprapubic pressure applied diagonally toward the anterior shoulder
options.rationale: McRoberts (hyperflexion of maternal hips) combined with suprapubic pressure resolves about 50% of shoulder dystocias and is the first step in HELPERR after Help is called. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Fundal pressure applied steadily for 60 seconds to push the anterior shoulder below symphysis
options.rationale: Fundal pressure worsens shoulder dystocia by impacting the shoulder against the symphysis and risks uterine rupture; contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Internal rotation manoeuvre (Woods corkscrew) using fingers to rotate the posterior shoulder
options.rationale: Internal rotation manoeuvres are part of HELPERR but come after McRoberts + suprapubic pressure; not the first step. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Symphysiotomy with deliberate division of the maternal pubic symphysis to widen the pelvis
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is essentially obsolete in Australian practice with severe maternal morbidity; not a recommended manoeuvre. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is a true obstetric emergency requiring rapid systematic intervention to prevent neonatal brachial plexus injury, hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy and maternal trauma. The HELPERR mnemonic structures intervention: H - call for Help; E - Evaluate for Episiotomy; L - Legs hyperflexed (McRoberts); P - suprapubic Pressure; E - Enter and rotate (internal rotation manoeuvres including Woods corkscrew, Rubin); R - Remove posterior arm; R - Roll over to all-fours. Fundal pressure is contraindicated as it worsens impaction. Symphysiotomy is essentially obsolete. The Zavanelli manoeuvre (cephalic replacement and caesarean delivery) is reserved as the last option after all others have failed. Time is critical - 5 minutes from head delivery is a soft upper limit before significant hypoxic injury risk.
key_points: HELPERR: Help, Episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), Pressure, Enter & rotate, Remove posterior arm, Roll
McRoberts + suprapubic pressure resolves ~50% of cases
Avoid fundal pressure (worsens dystocia)
Zavanelli = last resort with high maternal morbidity
Brachial plexus injury occurs in ~10% of shoulder dystocias
explanation.pearls: Delivery of the posterior arm has the highest success rate of any single manoeuvre (about 85% release) and should be attempted before internal rotation manoeuvres when McRoberts + suprapubic pressure fails.

stem: You are the obstetrics registrar in delivery suite. A 33-year-old G2P1 at 36+4 weeks ruptures membranes spontaneously at home and notes a small amount of bright red bleeding immediately. On arrival 30 minutes later her CTG shows a sinusoidal pattern with foetal heart rate 130 bpm. Vaginal examination shows clear blood-stained liquor. Her booking morphology scan at 20 weeks reported a low-lying placenta but no follow-up scan was performed. Maternal observations are stable and she is not in labour.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 116/72, HR 88; FHR 130 bpm, sinusoidal trace, no decelerations yet
data_table.Examination: Cervix 2 cm dilated, head 0 station, blood-stained clear liquor
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118; group and hold sent; Kleihauer pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Allow expectant management with continuous CTG and oxygen via face mask at 10 L/min
options.rationale: Vasa praevia exsanguinates the foetus within minutes; expectant management is contraindicated when the CTG shows sinusoidal pattern with bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG APH Guideline)

options.text: Urgent transabdominal ultrasound to identify vasa praevia and placental site before delivery
options.rationale: Sinusoidal CTG and bleeding post-SROM is presumptive vasa praevia; delaying delivery for confirmatory imaging risks foetal exsanguination. (Source: RANZCOG APH Guideline)

options.text: Category 1 emergency caesarean section with neonatal team in attendance for resuscitation
options.rationale: Sinusoidal CTG with vaginal bleeding immediately after SROM in a woman with previously low-lying placenta is classic vasa praevia until proven otherwise; foetal exsanguination is rapid and immediate caesarean is required. (Source: RANZCOG APH Guideline)

options.text: Augment labour with oxytocin and aim for rapid vaginal delivery within the next hour
options.rationale: Augmentation of labour with foetal exsanguination from suspected vasa praevia delays definitive control of haemorrhage; immediate caesarean is required. (Source: RANZCOG APH Guideline)

options.text: Send urgent foetal Kleihauer test on maternal blood to confirm foetal origin before delivery
options.rationale: Apt or Kleihauer testing to identify foetal blood is too slow when CTG already shows sinusoidal pattern indicating active foetal blood loss. (Source: RANZCOG APH Guideline)

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia describes foetal vessels (either from a velamentous cord or between placental lobes in a succenturiate placenta) running through the membranes over the cervix, vulnerable to rupture at SROM and resulting in catastrophic foetal exsanguination. Diagnosis is ideally made antenatally via transvaginal Doppler in women with risk factors (low-lying placenta, succenturiate lobe, velamentous insertion, multiple pregnancy, IVF). Antenatal diagnosis allows planned caesarean at 35 to 36 weeks with corticosteroids. Acute presentation (SROM + vaginal bleeding + sinusoidal CTG) requires Category 1 caesarean within 30 minutes; foetal mortality without immediate delivery exceeds 50%. Apt test or Kleihauer can confirm foetal origin retrospectively but should not delay delivery.
key_points: Vasa praevia: foetal vessels over the cervix, exsanguination at SROM
Risk factors: velamentous insertion, succenturiate lobe, IVF, multiple, low-lying placenta
Antenatal Doppler diagnosis enables planned CS at 35-36 weeks
Acute presentation: sinusoidal CTG + bleeding + SROM = Cat 1 CS
Foetal mortality >50% without immediate delivery
explanation.pearls: Sinusoidal CTG with foetal anaemia shows a smooth sine wave of 5 to 15 bpm amplitude at 2 to 5 cycles/min and is essentially pathognomonic of acute foetal blood loss until proven otherwise.

stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 67-year-old man via ED with two days of dysuria, rigors, and confusion. He has a background of type 2 diabetes on metformin. On arrival he is tachycardic, tachypnoeic, hypotensive, confused and mottled. Urinalysis shows nitrites and leukocytes. He has received 500 mL of 0.9% sodium chloride en route. Initial bloods include a lactate of 4.8 mmol/L.

data_table.Examination: Mottled extremities, dry mucous membranes, mild suprapubic tenderness
data_table.Investigations: VBG: lactate 4.8, pH 7.30, HCO3 18, glucose 11.2; FBC: WCC 18.4, Hb 138, plt 122; EUC: Cr 168 (baseline 92); CRP 240; urine: nitrites/leukocytes/blood positive

lead_in: Which intervention is the highest priority within the next hour?

options.text: Insert central venous catheter for CVP monitoring before commencing fluid resuscitation now
options.rationale: Central access and CVP monitoring should not delay initial fluid bolus and antibiotic; goal-directed CVP targets are no longer mandated by Surviving Sepsis. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast to identify the source before any antibiotic is given
options.rationale: Antibiotics should not be delayed for imaging; broad-spectrum antibiotic within 1 hour for septic shock is the priority. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis Pathway)

options.text: Start IV noradrenaline immediately at 0.05 mcg/kg/min before completion of fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is added after initial 30 mL/kg crystalloid if MAP <65 persists; starting vasopressor without fluid is not the first step. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis Pathway)

options.text: IV crystalloid 30 mL/kg with blood cultures then IV antibiotics within the first hour now
options.rationale: Surviving Sepsis hour-1 bundle and ANZICS recommend lactate measurement, blood cultures before antibiotics if no delay, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, and 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4 within the first hour. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis Pathway; Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone 200 mg before commencement of fluid resuscitation now
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is considered if vasopressor-refractory shock after adequate fluid; it is not the first step in the hour-1 bundle. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis Pathway)

explanation.summary: Australian sepsis pathway (ANZICS, Sepsis Australia) and Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle: measure lactate, obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, administer broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, begin 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4, and start vasopressors if hypotension persists during or after resuscitation to maintain MAP >=65. Source control should be pursued within 6 to 12 hours when feasible. For suspected urosepsis with septic shock, empirical antibiotic typically piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone plus gentamicin per local antibiogram. Reassessment at 3 to 6 hours guides further fluid or vasopressor titration. Lactate clearance is a useful marker of response.
key_points: Hour-1: lactate, cultures, broad-spectrum abx, 30 mL/kg crystalloid
Vasopressors if MAP <65 after fluid (target MAP >=65)
Source control within 6-12 hours when feasible
Hydrocortisone for vasopressor-refractory shock only
Lactate clearance is a marker of response
explanation.pearls: Each hour delay in appropriate antibiotic for septic shock increases in-hospital mortality by approximately 7%, which is the basis for the hour-1 bundle.

stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 56-year-old previously well man with 12 hours of severe headache, neck stiffness, fever and confusion. He has no rash. He has had no recent travel, no animal exposure and no immunosuppression. The ED has obtained IV access and is preparing to do an LP. He has no focal neurology and no papilloedema on fundoscopy.

data_table.Examination: Neck stiff to passive flexion, Kernig and Brudzinski positive, no rash, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.8, CRP 280, glucose 8.2, lactate 2.1, blood cultures sent; LP pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antimicrobial therapy?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12 hourly with IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4 hourly to cover Listeria empirically
options.rationale: Listeria cover (benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin) is added for age >=50 OR immunosuppression OR pregnancy; at 56 with no risk factors this is borderline but the dexamethasone is the critical first dose addition. The cleanest answer per eTG covers pneumococcus + meningococcus, with the Listeria addition for age >=50 specifically. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4 hourly with IV dexamethasone 10 mg 6 hourly given for 4 days
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin alone does not cover ceftriaxone-sensitive but penicillin-resistant pneumococcus; ceftriaxone is the empirical choice in Australian guidelines. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV cefotaxime 2 g 6 hourly with vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose IV ceftriaxone alternative
options.rationale: Vancomycin is added if highly penicillin-resistant pneumococcus is suspected (overseas exposure) but is not standard empirical in Australia where resistance rates are low. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV meropenem 2 g 8 hourly with IV dexamethasone 10 mg 6 hourly until cultures available
options.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for ceftriaxone allergy or specific resistance patterns; ceftriaxone is first-line empirical in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12 hourly with IV dexamethasone 10 mg 6 hourly given before or with antibiotic
options.rationale: eTG and ANZICS recommend IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12 hourly for empirical bacterial meningitis in adults, with dexamethasone 10 mg 6 hourly before or with first dose to reduce neurological sequelae from pneumococcal disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Australian empirical therapy for community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults is IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly, with dexamethasone 10 mg IV 6-hourly given before or with the first antibiotic dose to reduce neurological sequelae from pneumococcal meningitis. For patients >=50, immunosuppressed, alcohol misuse, or pregnant, add benzylpenicillin 2.4 g 4-hourly or amoxicillin 2 g 4-hourly for Listeria cover. Vancomycin is added when highly resistant pneumococcus is suspected (e.g. travel to high-resistance regions). Lumbar puncture should follow empirical antibiotic if delay is anticipated >30 minutes or if there are red flags for CT first (GCS <11, new focal neurology, seizures, papilloedema, immunocompromised). Public health notification and contact prophylaxis as required.
key_points: Empirical: ceftriaxone 2 g 12h + dexamethasone 10 mg 6h
Add Listeria cover if age >=50, immunosuppression, alcohol misuse, pregnant
Dexamethasone before/with first antibiotic dose - mortality and sequelae benefit
CT before LP if GCS <11, focal signs, seizures, papilloedema, immunocompromised
Public health notification + contact prophylaxis for meningococcal disease
explanation.pearls: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV 6-hourly for 4 days reduces severe hearing loss by about 50% in pneumococcal meningitis if given with or before the first antibiotic dose; the benefit is lost if delayed.

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 38-year-old woman with 36 hours of right loin pain, fever, rigors and dysuria. She has not vomited and is tolerating oral fluids. She is haemodynamically stable. Urinalysis shows nitrites and leukocytes. She has no diabetes, no urological history and is not pregnant. She has had no recent hospitalisation or instrumentation. She lives within 10 minutes of the hospital and has a partner at home.

data_table.Examination: Right renal angle tenderness, no peritonism, no costovertebral mass
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14.2, CRP 96, EUC: Cr 78, lactate 1.8; urinalysis: nitrites/leukocytes/blood

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7 days with ED safety net and 48 hour GP review
options.rationale: eTG recommends oral ciprofloxacin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for uncomplicated pyelonephritis in haemodynamically stable patients who can tolerate oral fluids; admission is not mandatory when there is no sepsis or complicating feature. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg four times daily for 10 days with GP review in 48 hours and clear safety net
options.rationale: Cefalexin is not recommended first-line for pyelonephritis in Australian eTG because of high E. coli resistance and poor renal tissue concentrations. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit for IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg single dose then oral switch to amoxicillin-clavulanate after
options.rationale: She does not meet admission criteria (no sepsis, no vomiting, tolerating oral, no complicating factors); admission is unnecessary and IV monotherapy gentamicin then oral switch is not first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit for IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g 8 hourly for 7 days as the broad-spectrum cover
options.rationale: Pip-tazo is unnecessarily broad for community-acquired uncomplicated pyelonephritis without urological history, instrumentation, or sepsis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg four times daily for 7 days with ED safety net and 48 hour review
options.rationale: Nitrofurantoin achieves urinary but not renal tissue concentrations and is unsuitable for pyelonephritis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Australian eTG recommends oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD for 7 days or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 BD for 14 days for uncomplicated pyelonephritis in haemodynamically stable patients tolerating oral intake. Admission is indicated for sepsis, persistent vomiting, pregnancy, urological abnormality, immunocompromise, age >65 with comorbidities, or social factors limiting safe discharge. Inpatient empirical therapy is gentamicin 4-5 mg/kg IV plus ampicillin or amoxicillin, or alternatively ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily, with oral switch when stable. Imaging (CT KUB or renal tract ultrasound) is indicated for recurrence, persistent fever after 48 hours, suspected obstruction or abscess. Recurrent pyelonephritis warrants urology referral.
key_points: eTG outpatient: ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD x 7d OR trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole x 14d
Admit for sepsis, vomiting, pregnancy, urological abnormality, immunocompromise
Inpatient empirical: gentamicin + amoxicillin, or ceftriaxone
Image if persistent fever >48 hours, recurrence, or suspected obstruction
Avoid cefalexin and nitrofurantoin for pyelonephritis
explanation.pearls: Fluoroquinolone-resistant E. coli rates have risen to about 15% in Australian community urine isolates, so clinical review at 48 to 72 hours to confirm response is essential.

stem: You are the surgical registrar called by ED to review a 52-year-old man with worsening leg redness over 18 hours. He has type 2 diabetes on metformin. He describes pain out of proportion to the visible erythema. He had a fall three days ago with a small abrasion on the calf. On examination there is a 15 cm area of erythema and tense induration with grey discolouration of a 4 cm central patch. He cannot weight-bear. His observations are tachycardic and febrile.

data_table.Examination: Calf erythema with central dusky discolouration, exquisite tenderness extending beyond visible margin, tense induration, no crepitus yet
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.6, Hb 132, CRP 320, Na 132, glucose 14.4, Cr 142 (baseline 88), CK 1840, lactate 3.4; X-ray: no gas in tissues

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: IV flucloxacillin 2 g 6 hourly with limb elevation and review at 24 hours for response
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin alone treats simple cellulitis but is inadequate for necrotising fasciitis where polymicrobial cover and surgical debridement are required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Urgent surgical debridement with broad-spectrum antibiotics including clindamycin for toxin
options.rationale: Pain out of proportion, tense induration with dusky discolouration, sepsis physiology and high LRINEC components (CRP 320, WCC 22, Cr 142, Na 132, glucose 14) point to necrotising fasciitis; urgent surgical debridement plus piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin + clindamycin (for toxin suppression) is the standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; ANZICS Necrotising Soft Tissue Infection)

options.text: MRI of the leg with contrast urgently to confirm necrotising fasciitis before any surgery
options.rationale: Imaging should not delay surgical exploration when clinical suspicion is high; MRI may help in uncertain cases but is not required before debridement here. (Source: ANZICS NSTI)

options.text: Compute LRINEC score and admit for IV ceftriaxone if score is 6 or higher with review
options.rationale: LRINEC is supportive but not sufficient to drive management; clinical features (pain out of proportion, dusky skin, sepsis) trump LRINEC and indicate immediate surgical referral. (Source: ANZICS NSTI)

options.text: IV vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading and admit under medical team for further investigations
options.rationale: Medical admission without surgical involvement is inappropriate; necrotising fasciitis mortality rises sharply with each hour delay to debridement. (Source: ANZICS NSTI)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency where mortality is determined more by time to debridement than by antibiotic choice. Classic features are pain out of proportion to visible inflammation, rapidly advancing erythema, tense induration, dusky or blistered skin, crepitus (late), and systemic toxicity. The LRINEC score (CRP, WCC, Hb, Na, Cr, glucose) supports diagnosis but does not exclude it; clinical features take precedence. Empirical antibiotics combine broad-spectrum (piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem) plus MRSA cover (vancomycin) plus toxin suppression (clindamycin or linezolid). Surgical exploration with debridement of all necrotic tissue is mandatory and may be repeated daily. Mortality is approximately 25% even with prompt treatment, rising to >50% if delayed beyond 24 hours.
key_points: Pain out of proportion is the key clinical sign
LRINEC score supports but does NOT exclude diagnosis
Triple antibiotic: pip-tazo + vancomycin + clindamycin (toxin suppression)
Surgical debridement is the definitive treatment; do not delay for imaging
Mortality ~25%; rises >50% if delayed >24 hours
explanation.pearls: Clindamycin in necrotising fasciitis suppresses streptococcal exotoxin synthesis, providing mortality benefit independent of bactericidal activity; it is added even when penicillin is in the regimen.

stem: You are the endocrinology registrar reviewing a 48-year-old woman with confirmed phaeochromocytoma on 24 hour fractionated metanephrines (urinary metanephrine 12 times upper limit) and a 4 cm right adrenal mass on CT. She has episodic hypertension peaking at 198/118 mmHg with palpitations, sweating and headaches. She is on no antihypertensive. She is scheduled for laparoscopic adrenalectomy in 14 days.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/104 supine, 144/92 standing, HR 88 supine, 110 standing
data_table.Examination: Mild orthostatic symptoms, no tremor, no goitre
data_table.Investigations: 24 hour fractionated metanephrines: metanephrine 12 x ULN; CT abdomen: 4 cm right adrenal mass; ECG: sinus rhythm, no LVH

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological preparation?

options.text: Amlodipine 5 mg daily and salt restriction with surgical referral in seven to fourteen days
options.rationale: Calcium channel blockers are adjunctive in phaeochromocytoma but do not replace alpha blockade; salt restriction is contraindicated as volume expansion is needed pre-surgery. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Metoprolol 25 mg twice daily for heart rate control then add alpha blocker once stable
options.rationale: Starting beta blocker before alpha blockade in phaeochromocytoma causes unopposed alpha vasoconstriction and can precipitate severe hypertensive crisis. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily titrated up over 7 to 14 days then add beta blocker if HR high
options.rationale: Australian endocrinology practice and international guidelines: alpha blockade with phenoxybenzamine for 7 to 14 days preoperatively to control hypertension and prevent intraoperative crisis; beta blocker is added ONLY after alpha blockade is established, to prevent unopposed alpha vasoconstriction. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline; eTG Endocrinology)

options.text: Combined alpha-beta blocker labetalol 200 mg twice daily as single agent control of BP and HR
options.rationale: Labetalol has predominantly beta over alpha blockade (ratio 7:1 oral) and is not adequate alpha blockade for phaeochromocytoma; phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin is preferred. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

options.text: Spironolactone 25 mg daily to address presumed primary aldosteronism with adrenal mass first
options.rationale: Diagnosis is confirmed phaeochromocytoma by elevated metanephrines; spironolactone is for primary aldosteronism and does not address catecholamine excess. (Source: Endocrine Society Phaeochromocytoma Guideline)

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma preoperative preparation requires alpha-adrenergic blockade for 7 to 14 days to control hypertension and restore intravascular volume. Phenoxybenzamine (non-selective irreversible) or doxazosin (selective alpha-1) are first-line. Beta blockers are added only after alpha blockade is established, to control reflex tachycardia and tachyarrhythmias; starting beta blocker first causes unopposed alpha vasoconstriction with risk of fatal hypertensive crisis. High-sodium diet (5 g/day) and oral fluids are recommended to restore volume depleted by catecholamine excess. BP target preoperatively is 130/80 mmHg seated and >90 mmHg systolic standing. Genetic testing (RET, VHL, SDH, NF1, MAX) is indicated for all phaeochromocytomas as 40% are hereditary. Surgical resection is curative in non-metastatic disease.
key_points: Alpha blockade FIRST (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) for 7-14 days
Beta blocker only AFTER alpha blockade established
High-sodium diet + fluid for volume expansion preoperatively
BP target 130/80 seated, >90 systolic standing
Genetic testing in all phaeochromocytomas (40% hereditary)
explanation.pearls: Phenoxybenzamine is started at 10 mg twice daily and titrated up by 10 to 20 mg every 2 to 3 days until target BP and mild orthostatic symptoms are achieved, indicating adequate blockade.

stem: You are the neurology registrar reviewing a 31-year-old woman with subacute optic neuritis confirmed by ophthalmology three weeks ago, now recovered with steroid. MRI brain today shows 3 periventricular and 2 juxtacortical T2 hyperintense lesions, one of which enhances with gadolinium. MRI spine shows 1 cervical cord lesion without enhancement. CSF (LP performed yesterday) shows oligoclonal bands present (CSF-specific). She has no prior neurological symptoms. There is no fever or recent infection.

data_table.Examination: Visual acuity 6/9 right, 6/6 left (recovered from 6/24); afferent pupillary defect right; remainder normal
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: 5 T2 lesions (3 periventricular, 2 juxtacortical), 1 gadolinium-enhancing; MRI spine: 1 cervical T2 lesion; CSF: oligoclonal bands positive (CSF-specific)

lead_in: Which McDonald 2024 criterion is met to confirm a diagnosis of multiple sclerosis?

options.text: Diagnosis cannot be made until a second clinical attack confirms dissemination in time
options.rationale: McDonald 2024 allows diagnosis after a single clinical attack with appropriate paraclinical evidence (DIS on MRI + CSF OCB or simultaneous enhancing/non-enhancing lesions). (Source: McDonald 2024)

options.text: Dissemination in space and time both met clinically without need for paraclinical evidence
options.rationale: She has only one clinical attack (optic neuritis); dissemination in time requires either a second clinical attack or paraclinical evidence. (Source: McDonald 2024)

options.text: Dissemination in time is met by simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on MRI scan
options.rationale: While the simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing pattern does meet DIT, the more direct criterion fulfilled here under McDonald 2024 is the CSF-specific oligoclonal bands acting as DIT substitute alongside DIS. Both are acceptable but the OCB pathway is the cleanest for clinical decision-making. (Source: McDonald 2024)

options.text: Dissemination in space met clinically; oligoclonal bands substitute for dissemination in time
options.rationale: McDonald 2024 criteria allow a single attack (here optic neuritis) plus DIS demonstrated on MRI plus CSF-specific oligoclonal bands to substitute for dissemination in time, confirming MS. (Source: McDonald 2024)

options.text: Diagnosis requires evoked potentials abnormality in addition to current criteria for confirmation
options.rationale: Evoked potentials are no longer required under McDonald 2024 if MRI and CSF criteria are met. (Source: McDonald 2024)

explanation.summary: The McDonald 2024 criteria diagnose MS based on dissemination in space (DIS) and dissemination in time (DIT). DIS requires >=1 T2 lesion in >=2 of 4 typical CNS regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). DIT can be met by a second clinical attack OR new T2 or enhancing lesion on follow-up MRI OR simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on the same scan OR CSF-specific oligoclonal bands (which substitute for DIT). Following confirmation, early initiation of disease-modifying therapy (DMT) is recommended. Australian MBS-funded options include glatiramer acetate, interferon beta, fingolimod, dimethyl fumarate, teriflunomide for moderate activity, and natalizumab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab, cladribine for high activity. JC virus serology should be checked before natalizumab.
key_points: McDonald 2024: DIS = T2 lesion in >=2 of 4 CNS regions
DIT = second attack, new MRI lesion, OR simultaneous Gd-enhancing + non-enhancing, OR CSF OCBs
CSF-specific OCBs substitute for DIT after single attack with DIS
Early DMT initiation reduces relapse rate and disability accumulation
JCV serology required before starting natalizumab
explanation.pearls: Ocrelizumab (anti-CD20) is the first DMT approved by TGA for primary progressive MS and is given as IV infusion every 6 months following an initial divided loading dose.

stem:
You are the ICU registrar called to a 72-year-old man on the cardiology ward, day 4 after anterior STEMI treated by primary PCI to LAD, now in shock. He has developed fever and worsening dyspnoea over 6 hours. Bedside echo shows anterior wall hypokinesis (similar to admission), LVEF 35%, no new tamponade, no new VSD, no severe MR. CXR shows new bilateral infiltrates.

Sputum is purulent. Lactate has risen from 1.4 to 3.8 over 6 hours. Cardiac index by non-invasive estimation is 2.0 L/min/m2. Mixed venous saturation by central line is 76%.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 84/52 (was 118/70 yesterday), HR 118, T 38.7, RR 28, SpO2 88% on 6 L/min, GCS 14
data_table.Examination: Warm peripheries despite hypotension, no peripheral oedema, no new murmur
data_table.Investigations: CXR: bilateral lower zone infiltrates new; troponin stable from yesterday; CRP 280; WCC 19; lactate 3.8; ScvO2 76%; CI 2.0
data_table.Medications: Aspirin, ticagrelor, atorvastatin, bisoprolol, ramipril, furosemide

lead_in: What is the most likely shock pattern and most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Cardiogenic shock from reinfarction; activate cath lab for repeat PCI within ninety minutes
options.rationale: Stable troponin and stable echo make reinfarction unlikely; fever and infiltrates point to sepsis. (Source: NHFA STEMI Guideline)

options.text: Cardiogenic shock from late stunning post-STEMI; start dobutamine and arrange repeat angiography
options.rationale: Echo shows stable LV function and no mechanical complication; warm peripheries with elevated ScvO2 are not typical of cardiogenic shock. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis Pathway)

options.text: Hypovolaemic shock from gastrointestinal bleed on dual antiplatelet; transfuse and pause DAPT
options.rationale: No evidence of bleeding; fever, purulent sputum and infiltrates point to infective rather than hypovolaemic cause. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis Pathway)

options.text: Obstructive shock from PE post-myocardial infarction; CT pulmonary angiogram and anticoagulate
options.rationale: Bilateral consolidation with purulent sputum and fever fit infective rather than embolic pattern; PE typically shows oligaemic lung fields. (Source: BMJ Best Practice)

options.text: Septic shock from hospital-acquired pneumonia; IV fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics now
options.rationale: Warm peripheries despite hypotension, fever, new infiltrates, purulent sputum, raised lactate and elevated mixed venous saturation (76% suggests distributive component) point to septic shock from HAP rather than cardiogenic deterioration; treat as septic shock with IV crystalloid and empirical antipseudomonal cover. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis Pathway; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Distinguishing shock subtypes at the bedside requires integration of clinical signs, haemodynamic profile and bedside imaging. Cardiogenic shock typically has cold peripheries, low cardiac index, raised PCWP and low mixed venous saturation. Septic shock typically has warm peripheries, normal or high cardiac index after initial fluid, low SVR, and normal or elevated mixed venous saturation. Hypovolaemic shock has cold peripheries, low cardiac index, low PCWP, low ScvO2. Mixed shock is increasingly recognised in critically ill patients with multiple insults. Empirical hospital-acquired pneumonia therapy in Australia is typically piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin if MRSA risk, with anti-pseudomonal cover for HAP onset >=5 days. Reassessment of shock subtype after initial intervention with serial echo, lactate, and central venous oximetry is essential.
key_points: Septic shock: warm, high CI after fluid, low SVR, normal/high ScvO2
Cardiogenic: cold, low CI, raised PCWP, low ScvO2
Hypovolaemic: cold, low CI, low PCWP, low ScvO2
HAP empirical (Australian): pip-tazo +/- vancomycin if MRSA risk
Reassess shock subtype after initial intervention
explanation.pearls: Mixed venous oxygen saturation (ScvO2) >70% with hypotension and lactate elevation is classic for distributive shock; ScvO2 <60% favours cardiogenic or hypovolaemic patterns.
